section.title: Bodily Discharges
section.sectionRationale: This is the last of the purity laws before the Day of Atonement, the seam moving from the extraordinary case of skin disease to the utterly ordinary case of bodily discharges tied to reproduction and death, showing that no corner of daily life falls outside the holiness code.
section.thesis: God requires that Israel's ordinary bodily discharges, tied to reproduction, life, and death, be washed and, when prolonged, atoned for, teaching a people camped around a holy God's tent that even the everyday leakage of mortal life must be kept from defiling His dwelling place among them.
section.frameNote: Five movements: a man's chronic discharge; the temporary uncleanness of an emission of semen; a woman's monthly impurity; a woman's prolonged discharge and its atoning offering; and the closing purpose, keeping Israel separate from uncleanness so they do not defile the tabernacle in their midst.

verses.text: And the LORD said to Moses and Aaron,
“Say to the Israelites, ‘When any man has a bodily {{zav|discharge}}, the discharge is unclean.
This uncleanness is from his discharge, whether his body allows the discharge to flow or blocks it. So his discharge will bring about uncleanness.
Any bed on which the man with the discharge lies will be unclean, and any furniture on which he sits will be unclean.
Anyone who touches his bed must wash his clothes and bathe with water, and he will be unclean until evening.
Whoever sits on furniture on which the man with the discharge was sitting must wash his clothes and bathe with water, and he will be unclean until evening.
Whoever touches the body of the man with a discharge must wash his clothes and bathe with water, and he will be unclean until evening.
If the man with the discharge spits on one who is clean, that person must wash his clothes and bathe with water, and he will be unclean until evening.
Any saddle on which the man with the discharge rides will be unclean.
Whoever touches anything that was under him will be unclean until evening, and whoever carries such things must wash his clothes and bathe with water, and he will be unclean until evening.
If the man with the discharge touches anyone without first rinsing his hands with water, the one who was touched must wash his clothes and bathe with water, and he will be unclean until evening.
Any clay pot that the man with the discharge touches must be broken, and any wooden utensil must be rinsed with water.
When the man has been cleansed from his discharge, he must count off seven days for his cleansing, wash his clothes, and bathe himself in fresh water, and he shall be clean.
On the eighth day he is to take two turtledoves or two young pigeons, come before the LORD at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting, and give them to the priest.
The priest is to sacrifice them, one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering. In this way the priest will make {{kipper|atonement}} for the man before the LORD because of his discharge.
When a man has an emission of semen, he must bathe his whole body with water, and he will be unclean until evening.
Any clothing or leather on which there is an emission of semen must be washed with water, and it will remain unclean until evening.
If a man lies with a woman and there is an emission of semen, both must bathe with water, and they will remain unclean until evening.
When a woman has a discharge consisting of blood from her body, she will be unclean due to her {{niddah|menstruation}} for seven days, and anyone who touches her will be unclean until evening.
Anything on which she lies or sits during her menstruation will be unclean,
and anyone who touches her bed must wash his clothes and bathe with water, and he will be unclean until evening.
Whoever touches any furniture on which she was sitting must wash his clothes and bathe with water, and he will be unclean until evening.
And whether it is a bed or furniture on which she was sitting, whoever touches it will be unclean until evening.
If a man lies with her and her menstrual flow touches him, he will be unclean for seven days, and any bed on which he lies will become unclean.

When a woman has a discharge of her blood for many days at a time other than her menstrual period, or if it continues beyond her period, she will be unclean all the days of her unclean discharge, just as she is during the days of her menstruation.
Any bed on which she lies or any furniture on which she sits during the days of her discharge will be unclean, like her bed during her menstrual period.
Anyone who touches these things will be unclean; he must wash his clothes and bathe with water, and he will be unclean until evening.
When a woman is cleansed of her discharge, she must count off seven days, and after that she will be ceremonially clean.
On the eighth day she is to take two turtledoves or two young pigeons and bring them to the priest at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
The priest is to sacrifice one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering. In this way the priest will make atonement for her before the LORD for her unclean discharge.

units.label: Kept separate, so the tabernacle is not defiled

verses.text: You must keep the children of Israel separate from their uncleanness, so that they do not die by defiling My {{mishkan|tabernacle}}, which is among them.
This is the law of him who has a discharge, of the man who has an emission of semen whereby he is unclean,
of a woman in her menstrual period, of any male or female who has a discharge, and of a man who lies with an unclean woman.’”

zav.gloss: One who has a flow or discharge, from a root meaning to flow or gush. The word carries no moral weight in itself; it is applied here to bodily discharge and elsewhere to the land flowing with milk and honey, taking its meaning entirely from context.

kipper.gloss: To atone, cover, or make amends. Used at the chapter's turning points to close each case of discharge, tying even an ordinary bodily condition, not itself a sin, to the same covering that dealt with moral guilt elsewhere in Leviticus.

niddah.gloss: Menstrual impurity, from a root meaning to exclude or separate. The word later expands in the prophets into a picture of moral defilement, as when Israel's idolatry is likened to a woman's niddah in Ezekiel 36:17.

mishkan.gloss: Dwelling place, tabernacle, from the root shakan, to dwell. The whole chapter's concern is not abstract hygiene but the concrete fact that a holy God actually dwells among an ordinarily unclean people.

basic.context: This chapter regulates ordinary bodily discharges: chronic issues, emissions of semen, monthly periods, and abnormal bleeding. None of these are sins in themselves, but they render a person ceremonially unclean for a time, requiring washing, and in prolonged cases, an offering.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a ceremonial purity law for Israel's camp gathered around the tabernacle, not a claim that bodies or sexuality are shameful, and not a command binding on Christians today. It teaches that even ordinary bodily life, marked by reproduction and mortality, needed careful boundaries around a holy God's presence.
basic.originalLanguages: The Hebrew zav names one with a flow; niddah names a woman's monthly impurity and later becomes a byword for moral defilement. Kipper, atonement, appears at the chapter's turning points, tying bodily uncleanness to the same covering that dealt with sin.
basic.typology: Washing and waiting until evening pictures a cleansing these rituals could only symbolize; an offering brought for a bodily condition that was never itself sin shows that Israel's atonement reached further than moral guilt, anticipating a Savior whose cleansing reaches every corner of human frailty.
basic.christ: Jesus lets a woman with a twelve year discharge of blood touch Him, exactly the person this law separated from the camp, and instead of becoming unclean Himself, He makes her clean, reversing the flow of defilement into a flow of healing, Mark 5:25-34.
basic.god: God is holy and near at once: near enough that ordinary human bodies, in birth, sex, and blood, must be considered in relation to His presence, yet gracious in providing everyday washing, not permanent exclusion, as the remedy.
basic.crossReferences: Mark 5:25-34 and Luke 8:43-48 show Jesus healing the woman with the flow of blood this very law describes. Ezekiel 36:17 uses niddah of Israel's moral impurity. Hebrews 9:13-14 argues from these external washings to the deeper cleansing of the conscience in Christ.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's careful boundaries around ordinary bodily life foreshadow the deeper truth that all human existence, not only visible sin, needs the cleansing God provides. That cleansing culminates when Christ touches the unclean and makes them whole, and is complete in the washing and sanctification believers now have in Him.

context.literary: This is the last of the purity laws in chapters 11 through 15, before the high point of chapter 16, the Day of Atonement. It moves from the extraordinary case of skin disease to the utterly ordinary case of bodily discharges, showing that no corner of daily life falls outside the holiness code.
context.historical: In the wilderness camp, with the tabernacle pitched at its center, matters we might consider merely private or medical carried a corporate dimension: an unclean person moving through the camp was genuinely moving close to God's tent.
context.canonical: This chapter's washing rituals become part of the larger biblical pattern in which physical cleansing signifies, and later gives way to, inward cleansing, promised in the prophets and fulfilled in the gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To fence the tabernacle from the ordinary uncleanness of bodily life, and to give Israel repeated, practical ways back to cleanness, so that God could go on dwelling in their midst.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a ceremonial purity law for the old covenant camp gathered around the tabernacle; it prescribes ritual washing and offerings, not permanent moral or hygienic instruction for the church today, compare Mark 7:19 and Acts 10:15. Its abiding truth is that a holy God's nearness required, and requires, careful, costly provision for cleansing.

debates.question: Does this law imply that bodily functions or sexuality are shameful or sinful?

views.label: Ceremonial impurity, not moral sin
views.case: The text never calls the discharge a sin; it is treated much like touching a corpse elsewhere in Leviticus, a ritual state requiring washing, not repentance, before approaching the tabernacle.

views.label: A deliberate association with mortality
views.case: Others note these are precisely the bodily events most tied to procreation and to the slow ebbing of life, so the law visibly reminds Israel of mortality and the fall every time these ordinary things occur.

debates.bearing: Either way, the law does not shame the body or sexuality as such; it marks a boundary between ordinary human life, touched by death's shadow, and the holy God who dwells among them.

study.originalLanguages: The Hebrew zav, one who flows, names the man's chronic condition without any moral freight, its root simply meaning to flow or gush. Niddah names the woman's monthly impurity and later becomes the prophets' word for moral defilement. Kipper, atonement, closes both the man's case and the woman's, tying bodily uncleanness to the same covering word used of sin offerings throughout Leviticus.

typology.fulfillment: A daily, repeatable picture of a cleansing these rituals could only symbolize, fulfilled once for all in the washing of regeneration through Christ, Titus 3:5.

typology.type: The woman with a chronic discharge of blood
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled when Jesus lets such a woman touch Him and, instead of being defiled, heals her, Mark 5:25-34, reversing the very logic of this chapter.

study.christ: This chapter separated anyone with a bodily discharge from the camp and from contact with others. Mark 5:25-34 and Luke 8:43-48 tell of a woman with exactly this condition for twelve years, who touches Jesus in desperate faith. Rather than becoming unclean Himself, Jesus reverses the flow: power goes out from Him, and she is healed and declared whole. What this law could only manage through separation, Christ accomplishes through contact and cleansing.
study.god: God's holiness is not aloof from ordinary human bodies; He legislates in detail for reproduction, sex, and bleeding, because He actually dwells among a people who experience these things. Yet He does not banish the unclean permanently; He provides washing, waiting, and, where needed, atonement, showing that His holiness is served by nearness and remedy, not by exclusion for its own sake.

crossReferences.note: Jesus heals a woman with a twelve year discharge of blood, the very condition this chapter addresses, without becoming unclean Himself.
Israel's sin is compared to a woman's niddah, showing how this chapter's vocabulary became a picture of moral impurity.
If the blood of bulls and goats and the sprinkling of unclean persons sanctified for the cleansing of the flesh, how much more will Christ's blood cleanse the conscience.

study.oneStory: This chapter's careful boundaries around ordinary bodily life, birth, sex, blood, and death's shadow, foreshadow the deeper truth that all human existence, not only deliberate sin, needs the cleansing God provides. That cleansing culminates when Christ lets the unclean touch Him and makes them whole, and is now complete in the washing and sanctification believers have in Him.

quiz.q: What made a man's bodily discharge ceremonially significant in this chapter?

options.text: It rendered him, and anything he touched or sat on, unclean until washed.
options.note: Verses 2-12 spell out this spreading uncleanness in detail.

options.text: It meant he had sinned against God.
options.note: The text never treats the discharge itself as sin, only as a ritual uncleanness.

options.text: It only affected the man himself, not his surroundings.
options.note: Verses 4-12 show the uncleanness spreading to beds, seats, and those who touch him.

options.text: It had no bearing on his daily life at all.
options.note: The whole chapter addresses exactly this concern in careful detail.

quiz.q: What was required on the eighth day after a discharge had ended?

options.text: Two turtledoves or two young pigeons, one for a sin offering and one for a burnt offering.
options.note: Verses 14-15 and 29-30 both specify this offering.

options.text: A single lamb sacrificed at the tabernacle.
options.note: The text specifies two birds, not one lamb.

options.text: Forty days of separation from the camp.
options.note: The period specified is seven days of counting toward cleansing, then an eighth day offering, not forty days.

options.text: No offering was required at all.
options.note: Verses 14-15 explicitly require an offering.

quiz.q: How long was a woman considered unclean during her ordinary monthly period, according to this law?

options.text: Seven days.
options.note: Verse 19 states this directly.

options.text: Until she gave birth.
options.note: That concerns childbirth, addressed in chapter 12, a separate law.

options.text: Forty or eighty days.
options.note: Those figures concern purification after childbirth, not the ordinary monthly period.

options.text: She was never considered unclean.
options.note: Verse 19 explicitly states she is unclean for seven days.

quiz.q: What was God's stated purpose for these laws, according to verse 31?

options.text: To keep Israel separate from uncleanness so they would not die by defiling the tabernacle among them.
options.note: Verse 31 states this explicitly.

options.text: To punish Israel for committing sin.
options.note: The chapter addresses ordinary bodily conditions, not punishment for wrongdoing.

options.text: To separate Israel culturally from other nations.
options.note: True elsewhere in the law, but verse 31 specifically names defiling the tabernacle as the concern here.

options.text: No purpose is given for these laws.
options.note: Verse 31 explicitly states the purpose.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament pick up the picture of a woman's bodily discharge from this chapter?

options.text: Jesus lets a woman with a twelve year flow of blood touch Him, and instead of becoming unclean, He heals her.
options.note: Mark 5:25-34 and Luke 8:43-48 record this directly.

options.text: The New Testament simply abolishes any concept of purity whatsoever.
options.note: This overstates the point; the Gospels show Jesus reversing the flow of defilement in one healing, not merely erasing all purity categories.

options.text: The New Testament never refers back to this chapter.
options.note: The Gospel accounts of the woman with the flow of blood directly echo this very law.

options.text: This chapter has no connection to the Gospels at all.
options.note: See Mark 5:25-34 for a direct connection.

questions: This chapter treats ordinary, unchosen bodily events as needing careful attention before a holy God. Where do you quietly assume that only your deliberate sins matter to God, and not the frailty of your body and circumstances?
Washing and waiting until evening was a small, repeatable, everyday act of returning to cleanness. What ordinary, repeatable habits help you return to God after you sense distance from Him?
Jesus let the very woman this law separated touch Him, and He was not defiled, she was healed. Where do you need to bring your uncleanness or shame to Jesus rather than staying at a distance?

God's stated reason for these laws was so He could keep dwelling among a people prone to uncleanness. How does knowing God chooses ongoing nearness, not distance, change how you approach Him after failure?

This law made no one a permanent outsider; each uncleanness had its own ordinary path back to cleanness. Who do you know who feels permanently disqualified, and how might you extend the hope Christ offers?

translationNotes.note: Bodily discharge translates a phrase built on the Hebrew root zub or zav, describing an abnormal genital flow; the same root describes a land flowing with milk and honey, so the word itself carries no shame, only meaning to flow.
Menstruation translates niddah, a word that later expands in the prophets into a broader picture of impurity and moral defilement, for example Ezekiel 36:17.

Defiling My tabernacle uses mishkan, dwelling place, the same root that names the tabernacle throughout Exodus; the concern is not abstract ritual but the actual nearness of God's presence in the camp.
